sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 40-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of right shoulder pain that began after a period of intense physical activity in the backyard, including lifting and manual labor.
The pain is located on the outside of the shoulder and is exacerbated by movement, but not constant.
He reports some soreness to touch and tenderness at the great tuberosity of the humerus.
He has a history of minor shoulder issues, including a previous injury to the same shoulder while playing baseball in his youth.
He denies numbness in his arm or hands.
He has been taking Advil and icing the area for relief.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of minor shoulder issues, including a previous injury to the right shoulder.
Review of systems: The patient denies numbness in his arm or hands.
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen for pain relief.
Objective:

Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are within normal limits, including a temperature of 97.8°F, heart rate of 62 beats per minute, respirations of 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure of 116/84 mmHg, and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the great tuberosity of the humerus.
There is no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
He has a good hand grip and normal neurovascular exam results, including capillary refill of less than 3 seconds and intact sensation to touch.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray is negative for fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, an injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; status: confirmed; Chronic pain; status: active; Dehydration; status: suspected.
Plan:
I ordered an MRI to further evaluate the patient's shoulder.
I referred the patient to physical therapy for 6-8 weeks to address the rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I instructed the patient to continue taking Tylenol for pain relief.
If symptoms do not improve, I will consider a steroid injection for the shoulder.
I advised the patient to try soaking their feet in Epsom salts to help with foot soreness.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in 6-8 weeks to reassess their symptoms and progress with physical therapy.
